did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an objective system based on multiple connected neural networks for melanoma detection in dermatology. The method includes a first level of subjective classifiers: perceptron coupled with color local binary patterns (LBP), perceptron coupled with color histograms of oriented gradients (HOG), a generative adversarial network (GAN) for segmentation coupled with the ABCD rule, ResNet, and AlexNet. A second-level objective classifier (back-propagation perceptron) combines these decisions. The system was trained and tested on ISIC 2019 and PH2 databases, achieving 97.5% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a new objective system based on multiple connected neural networks for melanoma detection. It details specific methods (perceptrons, GAN, ResNet, AlexNet), datasets used (ISIC 2019, PH2), and reports original results (97.5% accuracy, 97.47% F1 score), distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: Melanoma Detection Using an Objective System Based on Multiple Connected Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study proposes an objective system for melanoma detection using multiple connected neural networks to support dermatologists in diagnosing skin cancer. The architecture employs a two-level classification approach, where five subjective classifiers (perceptrons with LBP and HOG, a GAN-segmented ABCD rule classifier, ResNet, and AlexNet) analyze lesion features at the first level. A second-level objective classifier, a back-propagation perceptron, combines these decisions to produce the final diagnosis. The system was trained and evaluated on the ISIC 2019 and PH2 dermoscopic image databases. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 97.5% and an F1 score of 97.47%, outperforming individual classifiers and previous literature results.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The system distinguishes melanoma from non-melanoma lesions. The ISIC 2019 dataset used for training/testing contains 25,331 lesion images including melanoma.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The paper states the system distinguishes melanomas from benign lesions. The ISIC 2019 dataset includes various skin diseases such as melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, etc., which fall under this category in the context of the binary melanoma vs. non-melanoma task.
Mentioned as one of the 9 classes of skin diseases present in the ISIC 2019 database, but not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the binary melanoma detection task described.
Mentioned as one of the 9 classes of skin diseases present in the ISIC 2019 database, but not explicitly isolated as a separate experimental class in the binary melanoma detection task described.
Mentioned in the context of related work [29] regarding classification of skin lesions, not as a primary class in this paper's experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnosis of melanoma vs. non-melanoma
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task where the system predicts whether a lesion is a melanoma (Me) or non-melanoma (NMe). The final objective classifier (back-propagation perceptron) outputs a decision based on desired outputs of 1 for melanoma and 0 for non-melanoma. The system achieves 97.5% accuracy and 97.47% F1 score on this classification task.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public database obtained from the dermatology department of Pedro Hispano Hospital, Matosinhos, Portugal. Images were acquired using a Tuebinger Mole Analyzer with 20x magnification. The database contains manual segmentation, clinical diagnosis, and identification of dermoscopic structures by expert dermatologists.

use.training: Used for training the system (combined with ISIC 2019). Specifically, 200 images were used for subjective classifier training (SC training) after augmentation, and 80 images (40 Me, 40 NMe) were used for the final testing phase.
use.validation: Used for validation of subjective classifiers (combined with ISIC 2019). Specifically, 200 images were used for validation (establishing confidence coefficients) after augmentation.
use.testing: 80 images (40 melanoma, 40 non-melanoma) were used for the final testing phase of the global system.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: Part of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) database, developed by several medical institutions. It is a collection of multiple databases containing RGB images of various sizes, perspectives, and lighting conditions, acquired with different dermatoscope types. Most images have associated clinical metadata verified by recognized melanoma experts.

use.training: Used for training the system (combined with PH2). Specifically, 1000 images (500 Me, 500 NMe) were used for subjective classifier training (SC training) after augmentation to 2000 images.
use.validation: Used for validation of subjective classifiers (combined with PH2). Specifically, 1000 images were used for validation (establishing confidence coefficients) after augmentation to 200 images.
use.testing: 1000 images (500 Me, 500 NMe) were used for the final testing phase of the global system (specifically 320 augmented images from ISIC 2019 were mentioned in the testing phase description, but the total count used for testing across both datasets was 1000 images: 800 for PF training and 400 for testing, with 320 from ISIC and 80 from PH2 for the final test set of the global system).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate Accuracy (ACC) and F1 score by counting True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN).
To weight the output of subjective classifiers based on their accuracy calculated during the validation phase.
To adjust weights in the objective classifier (perceptron) by computing the difference between predicted and desired outputs.
To normalize the network predicted output between 0 and 1 for the objective classifier.
To determine the probability of melanoma detection using the ABCD rule, with a threshold of 5.45 indicating high suspicion.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and F1 scores on specific datasets (ISIC 2019 and PH2) without statistical significance testing.

SC1 (LBP-Perceptron).Note: Specific value not reported in text, but listed in Table 5

SC2 (HOG-Perceptron).Note: Specific value not reported in text, but listed in Table 5

SC3 (GAN-ABCD).Note: Specific value not reported in text, but listed in Table 5

SC4 (ResNet-101).Note: Specific value not reported in text, but listed in Table 5. Validation accuracy was 93.67%.

ISIC_2019_and_PH2.notes: The global system was tested on 400 images: 320 from ISIC 2019 (160 Me, 160 NMe) and 80 from PH2 (40 Me, 40 NMe). The results (97.5% ACC, 97.47% F1) are better than individual subjective classifiers and previous literature. The paper notes that ACC and F1 are close because the test set was balanced (equal number of Melanoma and Non-Melanoma cases).

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-connected neural network system with two levels of classification: five subjective classifiers (SC1-SC5) and one objective classifier (back-propagation perceptron).
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning approach used for ResNet-101 and AlexNet. ResNet-101 was originally trained on ImageNet (ILSVRC2015). AlexNet was designed by Krizhevsky et al. (implies ImageNet pretraining). GAN was trained on the specific dataset. Perceptrons were trained from scratch on the specific dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 × 224 × 3 pixels (for ResNet-101 and AlexNet). Other inputs depend on feature extraction (LBP/HOG histograms, ABCD rule metrics).

SC1.name: Color LBP-Perceptron
SC1.type: Perceptron coupled with color Local Binary Patterns (LBP)

SC2.name: Color HOG-Perceptron
SC2.type: Perceptron coupled with color Histograms of Oriented Gradients (HOG)

SC3.name: GAN-ABCD Classifier
SC3.type: Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for segmentation coupled with ABCD rule
SC3.details: GAN used for lesion segmentation (mask generation). ABCD rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter) used for classification based on segmented lesion. Confidence coefficient derived from pABCD and pGAN.

SC4.name: ResNet-101
SC4.structure: Residual blocks with skip connections (shortcuts) to solve vanishing/exploding gradients. Uses ReLU activation.
SC4.training_details: 6 epochs, 86 iterations per epoch, 516 total iterations. Validation accuracy: 93.67%.

SC5.name: AlexNet
SC5.activation: ReLU in convolutional layers, Softmax in output layer (modified for binary probability)

objective_classifier.name: Back-propagation Perceptron (PF)
objective_classifier.inputs: Outputs (xi) from the five subjective classifiers (SC1-SC5)
objective_classifier.calculation: Hidden layer output S = sum(xi * wi). Final output Po normalized via Sigmoid function: Po = 1 / (1 + exp(-S)).
objective_classifier.training: Back-propagation error (Er = Po - Do) used to adjust weights (wi). Desired output Do is 1 for Melanoma, 0 for Non-Melanoma.

phases: Training phase 1 for individual subjective classifiers (SC1-SC5)
Validation phase for individual subjective classifiers (to establish confidence coefficients ci)
Training phase for the final objective perceptron (PF)
training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly named for perceptrons; GAN uses separate optimizers for Generator and Discriminator. Back-propagation used for perceptron weight updates.

loss_function.Perceptrons: Back-propagation error (difference between predicted and desired output)

training_details.epochs: 6 epochs for ResNet-101 and AlexNet training.

classification_or_output_head.SC3: Product of pABCD and pGAN (probability of melanoma)
classification_or_output_head.SC4_SC5: Softmax layer modified to provide probability of belonging to Melanoma or Non-Melanoma classes
classification_or_output_head.Objective_Perceptron: Sigmoid function output (probability of melanoma), thresholded for final decision (Melanoma vs Non-Melanoma)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Perceptron coupled with color local binary patterns (LBP)
Perceptron coupled with color histograms of oriented gradients (HOG)
Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) coupled with ABCD rule
preprocessing_techniques: Image decomposition on color channels (R, G, B)
Lesion segmentation (using GAN for mask generation)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions using data augmentation to address dataset deficiencies such as small volume and imbalance. Specifically, the ISIC 2019 training set of 1000 images (500 Me, 500 NMe) was augmented to 2000 images for subjective classifier training. The PH2 database images were also augmented for training and validation phases.

A Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) was used for lesion segmentation. The generator created fake masks (FM) from real lesion images, which were used to generate segmentation masks. This process effectively augmented the feature representation for the ABCD rule classifier branch (SC3) by providing precise lesion boundaries.

conclusions: The proposed objective system based on multiple connected neural networks achieved an accuracy of 97.5% and an F1 score of 97.47% for melanoma detection.
The system's performance was superior to that of individual subjective classifiers and previous literature results.

The two-level classification architecture, combining five subjective classifiers (perceptron with LBP, perceptron with HOG, GAN-ABCD, ResNet, and AlexNet) with an objective back-propagation perceptron, effectively compensates for subjective errors.
The use of confidence coefficients derived from validation phases allows for the weighting of subjective classifier outputs, enhancing the objectivity of the final decision.

The system design facilitates easier transition between different databases, as changing the lesion database only requires retraining the final objective perceptron (phase 3) and testing (phase 4), eliminating the need for retraining individual subjective classifiers (phase 1).
The study acknowledges that the number of subjective classifiers used (five) was a compromise between cost and performance, and the optimal number remains an open question.

Future work is needed to address the use of more efficient methodologies for determining classifier evolution (e.g., ABCDE criterion instead of ABCD) and to replace subjective segmentation with appropriate manual segmentation.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced test set (400 images total: 320 from ISIC 2019 and 80 from PH2) which may not reflect real-world class imbalance or generalizability.
The paper acknowledges that the number of subjective classifiers (five) was a compromise between cost and performance, noting that the optimal number remains an unresolved theoretical problem.
Performance metrics (ACC and F1) are artificially close because the test set was balanced (equal number of melanoma and non-melanoma cases), which masks potential issues with class imbalance.

The system's adaptability to new databases is limited to retraining only the final objective perceptron, but the subjective classifiers were trained exclusively on ISIC 2019, potentially introducing dataset bias.
The study does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) to validate the superiority of the proposed method over previous literature.
The segmentation component uses a GAN, but the paper notes that subjective segmentation is a limitation and suggests future work should replace it with manual segmentation for higher accuracy.
The preprocessing steps (noise filtering, hair removal, etc.) are mentioned but not detailed, making reproducibility of the input data quality difficult to assess.

The claim of 'objective' classification is nuanced; the system combines 'subjective' classifiers (individual neural networks) via a final perceptron, and the term 'subjective' refers to the individual classifiers' specific feature focuses rather than human subjectivity.
